answer:
The translation of 'dharma' breaks down most painfully at three distinct moments across the three smritis, each exposing a different inadequacy in the English candidates:

**1. 'Duty' fails when dharma is cosmic order or legal norm** (Manusmrti, ch. 7)

In Manu 7.14–7.18, Daṇḍa (the rod of punishment) is identified as Dharma itself, created by the Lord to protect all beings: 'For his sake, the Lord in the beginning created Daṇḍa, his own son, as Dharma, the protector of all beings, formed of the spiritual luster of Brahma.' (p-73f910, Mn_7.14) and 'Daṇḍa rules all subjects, Daṇḍa alone protects them; Daṇḍa is awake when others sleep; the wise know Daṇḍa to be Dharma itself.' (p-48dedc, Mn_7.18). 'Duty' is hopeless here — the text is not telling anyone what they must do; it is identifying the coercive legal apparatus of the state as an ontological manifestation of cosmic right-order. 'Law' comes closer (Daṇḍa as enforceable norm), but the passage slips into cosmology mid-sentence ('formed of the spiritual luster of Brahma'), so 'law' in the secular sense also fails. What the passage is doing is simultaneously cosmic-theological AND juridical AND prescriptive — no single English word holds all three registers at once.

**2. 'Law' fails when dharma is existential virtue or inner character** (Viṣṇu Smṛti, ch. 2)

Viṣṇu Smṛti 2.16–2.17 enumerates a list — forbearance, truthfulness, self-control, purity, charity, restraint of the senses, non-violence, compassion, straightforwardness, freedom from greed — and concludes: 'And freedom from envy — this is declared to be the common dharma for all.' (p-00e45d, Vi_2.17). This is character ethics, not a legal code. Nothing about it resembles a statute, a rule enforceable by a court, or a positional obligation arising from caste. 'Law' would be absurd here — no legal system legislates 'freedom from envy.' 'Virtue' fits well: these are Aristotelian-style stable dispositions of character. But then Vi_2.4–2.8 (immediately preceding) says 'their duties (dharma) are as follows: The duty of a Brāhmaṇa is teaching; the duty of a Kṣatriya is constant practice with arms' (p-b44991, p-354ee5, p-f4e97b) — here the same word means positional role-obligation, i.e., 'duty' and not 'virtue'. Viṣṇu uses dharma in both senses within the same chapter, so a translator fixing on 'virtue' for Vi_2.17 must also apply it to Vi_2.5, where a Brāhmaṇa's 'virtue' is 'teaching' — which makes it sound like a personal excellence rather than a socially-enforced function.

**3. 'Virtue' fails when dharma is the highest soteriological practice** (Yājñavalkya Smṛti, ch. 1)

Yājñavalkya 1.8 declares: 'Of sacrifice, right conduct, sense-control, non-violence, giving, and the study of the Veda — of these actions, this is the highest dharma: the vision of the Self through yoga.' (p-1e45c4, Yj_1.8). If we render dharma as 'virtue,' the verse says the highest virtue is mystical yogic vision of the Ātman. But 'virtue' imports an Aristotelian/Christian framework of moral excellence, which misses the specifically soteriological function: the dharma here is not an ethical quality but a salvific practice — the means of liberation. Similarly, the same chapter's definition-verse (Yj_1.7) roots dharma in 'desire born of right resolve' alongside the Vedas and virtuous conduct (p-7d795b), giving it a volitional-intentional dimension that 'virtue' (a trait) cannot carry. And Yj_1.354 says 'dharma was created of old by Brahmā in the form of punishment' (p-ff5556) — the same text where dharma just meant yogic liberation now means coercive cosmic law.

**Summary of breakdown points:**
- 'Duty' picks up role-obligation (Vi_2.4–2.8) but collapses before the cosmological-legal identification of Daṇḍa with Dharma (Mn_7.14–7.18) and before the soteriological sense (Yj_1.8).
- 'Law' picks up the juridical-coercive sense (Mn_7.14–7.18; Yj_1.354) but cannot carry the virtue-list (Vi_2.16–2.17) or the liberation-path sense (Yj_1.8).
- 'Virtue' picks up the character-ethics list (Vi_2.16–2.17) and part of the conduct-and-conduct-of-the-virtuous definition (Mn_2.6; Yj_1.7), but is entirely wrong for the legal-coercive (Mn_7.18; Yj_1.354) and role-obligation senses (Vi_2.5–2.8).

The choice breaks down most painfully in Manu ch. 7 and Yājñavalkya ch. 1, where dharma slides within the same passage from cosmic ontology to coercive law to liberation-practice — the three English words cover only one register each and leave the other two stranded.

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Mn_1.26: dharma/non-dharma as cosmic pair; Mn_1.81-1.86: dharma as four-footed cosmic principle varying by age; Mn_1.87-1.91: dharma as varna-specific duty

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Mn_7.14–7.18: Danda identified as Dharma itself, protector of beings — dharma as coercive legal-cosmic force; p-73f910, p-48dedc

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Mn_2.6: dharma's root sources (Veda, Smriti, virtuous conduct, self-satisfaction); Mn_2.1: dharma as what the heart assents to — p-d70b97, p-c52281

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Yj_1.6-1.9: dharma as mark of right gift, root in Vedas/conduct/self-resolve, highest dharma = yogic vision of Self (p-1e45c4); Yj_1.354: dharma created by Brahma as punishment (p-ff5556); Yj_1.358: deviating from own dharma is punishable (p-caadbb)

tool_calls.name: read_chapter
tool_calls.result_summary: Vi_1.5: Vishnu described as 'composed of dharma and truth' (p-8081a1); Vi_1.54: Vishnu as 'knower of dharma and adharma, embodiment of dharma, source of dharma' (p-aad632)

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Vi_2.4-2.8: dharma=specific varna duty (p-b44991, p-354ee5, p-f4e97b, p-6910e1, p-f34593); Vi_2.16-2.17: dharma=common virtue list for all, including freedom from envy (p-00e45d)

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Confirmed Mn_7.14 (p-73f910) and Mn_7.18 (p-48dedc): Danda as Dharma itself

tool_calls.name: get_passage
tool_calls.result_summary: Confirmed Yj_1.7 (p-7d795b): root of dharma includes self-resolve; Yj_1.8 (p-1e45c4): highest dharma = vision of Self through yoga
